title: When Your Therapist Underestimates Your Rage
dek: Your jaw locked mid-session. The therapist suggested another breathing exercise. You felt the old script rise: *you’re too much*. But below the smile was an oven-hot pressure in your sternum - something not hysterical, not irrational, but ancient and precise. This is about what happens when the room meant to hold your pain asks you to shrink it instead.
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. When Your Therapist Underestimates Your Rage is a place where the body and the spirit meet, and it asks for honest attention rather than control. In our experience, healthy anger is information about a boundary, a value, or a longing that has been crossed or unmet. This piece walks you through what to notice, what to try, and what to leave alone, in plain language and with real care.

--- *Last updated January 18, 2026.* The woman in the chair had been grinding her molars for seventeen minutes. The therapist across from her, a gentle-eyed man in a cardigan, kept a soothing cadence. He’d mentioned “cognitive reframing” twice. She’d nodded. She’d agreed that her boss was not actually her father, that her partner was not trying to abandon her, that the fury rising when her mother texted a single thumbs-up emoji might be disproportionate. Then, as he offered a handout on distress tolerance, she felt the base of her skull tighten like a fist. The room went slightly gray at the edges. She imagined, for a split second, hurling the glass paperweight on his desk through the window just to see something shatter outside her own body. She swallowed. She said, “Maybe you’re right. I just need to let it go.” Her body believed none of it. Her trapezius muscles were granite. Her hands, folded in her lap, had started a tiny tremor she hid by pressing them flat. This woman had survived a childhood where no one ever said sorry. She had swallowed decades of slights, betrayals, and moments so small they didn’t have names - only a cumulative heat that could, on any given Tuesday, feel like it might burn her from the inside out. And here she was, in a space she paid for, learning yet again that her rage was a behavior to manage, not a language to translate. --- ## The Room Where They Pathologize Your Heat Therapy rooms are strange biomes. They promise a container for the unspeakable, yet they often reproduce the very silencing they’re meant to repair. When a client’s anger surfaces, many well-intentioned clinicians reach for regulation first. They offer breathing protocols. They ask, “What’s underneath the anger?” - as if anger itself is never the primary event, only a lid for sadness or fear. This is a therapeutic half-truth, and it can do quiet violence. You might know the sensation: the words come out of you in a burst, maybe too loud, maybe sarcastic, maybe coated in a kind of vocal crispness that makes the therapist’s face go slightly neutral. Their pen pauses. They tilt their head. You interpret it as judgment, and you’re often correct. The invisible assumption is that your anger is a distortion, a symptom, a sign that you’ve lost the plot of polite emotionality. What happens next is a micro-sized betrayal: you are nudged back toward the acceptable register of self-expression, the one that doesn’t frighten the caretaker. The body registers this as a second wound. Your throat catches on a shard of unsaid sentences. The space behind your eyes gets hot and dry, as if the tears you’re not allowed to show have evaporated into sharp dust. You might even thank your therapist for the insight, while your inner monologue razes villages. The therapeutic alliance frays not because you’re resistant, but because you’ve been offered a script that requires you to abandon the truest part of your nervous system: the part that knows something is badly wrong and has been wrong for a very long time. ## What Your Body Already Knows The body remembers what the treatment plan forgets. Long before you had language for injustice, you had a gut that churned when boundaries were crossed. You had a jaw that locked when you were told to be nice. You had shoulder blades that crawled up toward your ears every time you heard a caregiver’s footsteps in the hallway and braced for impact. That somatic wiring does not vanish with talk therapy or positive thinking. Harriet Lerner, in her decades of work on women and anger, made a simple, radical observation: anger is a signal, not a pathology. It points us toward areas where our selfhood has been compromised - where we’ve over-functioned, swallowed unfairness, or tolerated violation. When a therapist sidesteps that signal to focus on “softening” your reaction, they miss the entire point. Lerner writes that women, in particular, are taught from childhood to keep the peace, to avoid appearing “difficult.” Therapy can reinforce this if it becomes another place where you perform calmness to earn approval. The body, however, will not be gaslit. It will keep sending its bulletins: a hot neck flush when you’re condescended to, a hollow pit in your stomach when you’re not believed, a buzzing in your hands that says *move, fight, do something*. Thich Nhat Hanh, the Zen teacher, brought a different lens but a parallel respect for rage. He famously taught that anger is like a crying baby: you don’t smother it or ignore it; you hold it with mindfulness. “Taking care of your anger,” he said, means being present with it without judgment, breathing into the sensation until the tight knot in your chest unfurls just enough for you to see its shape. That’s not the same as the five-count inhale-exhale prescribed as a shutdown switch. It’s an invitation to sit in the fire without letting it consume you - a profound distinction many therapists elide. Karla McLaren, an empathic researcher who mapped the emotional lexicon, takes this even further. She insists that anger is the guardian of your boundaries, the emotion that rises when your sense of self or safety is breached. Her work flips the script on anger management: it’s not about controlling rage, but *honoring* it as the inner bouncer that says “No further.” If you bypass that message, you lose access to a core protective intelligence. When a therapist underestimates your rage, they aren’t just missing a diagnostic clue; they’re asking you to fire the one guard you have left. ## A Counterintuitive Reframe: Rage Is Not the Enemy Here’s a claim that might feel impossible at first: the rage you’ve been taught to fear is actually a form of clarity. The problem is not the heat itself. The problem is the decades of inadequate containers. When you were sixteen and your father humiliated you at the dinner table, you couldn’t scream then without punishment. When you were twenty-three and your partner cheated, you might have been told anger was “unforgiving.” When you were thirty-five and your doctor dismissed your pain, you channeled anger into self-blame. The rage stored in your tissues is not a monster; it’s the backlog of unsent letters. Niche researchers on somatic trauma have shown that unexpressed anger can become chronic hypervigilance, autoimmune flares, and a resting muscle tone that feels like wearing armor while trying to swim. The therapist who tells you to breathe before you have acknowledged what you need to say is, in effect, asking you to add another layer to the armor. The more counterintuitive path: let the rage speak first - in a safe enough way, with your full consciousness present - and then watch what changes in your breathing without being instructed. You can feel this in your own body when you finally say the unsayable. Maybe you scream into a pillow in your car after the session where you were misunderstood. The sound rips out of your throat, grainy and ugly. Your sinuses fill with pressure. Then, almost immediately, your shoulders drop half an inch. Your jaw unhinges slightly, and your teeth, which you’d been clenching all day, feel suddenly orphaned, floating in a mouth that can now taste air. This is the body remembering its own release valve. It doesn’t need a fifteen-minute guided meditation; it needs permission to finish a physiological sequence that was interrupted years ago. ## When Mindfulness Becomes a Bypass Somewhere along the way, spiritual language got weaponized against the genuinely furious. “Your anger is just your ego,” you might hear in certain circles. “You need to surrender.” “Holding onto resentment hurts only you.” These sentences, often delivered with a beatific smile, are not wisdom. They’re a form of emotional laziness dressed as enlightenment. They ask you to leapfrog the very emotional processing that makes surrender meaningful. Thich Nhat Hanh never said to bypass anger. He said to hold it, to cradle it with the tenderness of a mother, not to shove it under a meditation cushion. The difference is monumental. True mindfulness means arriving at your rage without a hidden agenda to make it disappear. It means letting the knot in your belly be exactly as dense as it is, letting the prickly heat on your skin spread without recoiling, letting the urge to break something exist without acting on it or denying it. That is not the same as the pop-culture, app-driven version of mindfulness that functions as a tranquillizer. For a product that can support this grounded, body-aware approach, you might keep a small bottle of [Gaia Herbs PRO Calm Restore](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BTMTSSZ6?tag=spankyspinola-20) nearby. Vetiver is an earthy, unglamorous scent - it doesn’t lift you into the clouds but anchors you into the floor, which is precisely where rage wants you to be. A drop rubbed into the soles of your feet before you sit with a difficult memory creates a sensory tether. It says: *you can be this angry and still stay in your own skin.* Any therapist or teacher who uses the language of presence to shut you up is not practicing presence. They’re practicing avoidance with a sacred gloss. And your body can tell the difference: when you’re genuinely being invited into mindful awareness, your breathing slows organically, from the bottom of your lungs, not from a top-down tightness that mimics a held breath. When you’re being spiritually bypassed, your stomach stays hard, your throat stays constricted, and a part of you wants to cry - not from sadness, but from the profound exhaustion of not being met. ## The Five-Minute Practice: Letting Rage Move Without Destroying You don’t need an hour. You don’t need a soundproof room. What you need is a commitment to not abandon yourself when the heat comes. This practice, inspired by somatic therapies and McLaren’s boundary work, takes under five minutes and requires only your own limbs and breath. Do it when you notice the telltale signs: a clenched rectum, a hot flush spreading up your neck, a sense that your hands want to curl into fists or claws. First, plant your feet firmly, hip-width. Slap the outside of your thighs with open palms, five times per side - stinging, rhythmic, jarring. This wakes up your skin and interrupts the freeze. Second, take one inhale through your nose, deep into your belly, and on the exhale, produce a low, sustained *voo* sound, like a foghorn. Feel the vibration in your chest and throat. Do this three times. Third, clench every muscle in your body - face, fists, glutes, toes - for a count of seven, then release with a forceful exhale through the mouth. Repeat twice. Finally, stand still. Notice the tingling in your hands, the shift in your inner volume. Say to yourself, not aloud, “I hear what you’re telling me.” Then, place a hand on the center of your chest and wait ten seconds. That’s it. This is not about catharsis as performance. It’s about allowing the motor sequence of anger to complete without it turning into a destructive act. The body’s response is often immediate: a sudden drool of saliva as the parasympathetic nervous system engages, a softening in the ocular muscles so your vision widens, a surprising wave of fatigue that tells you the vigilance cell has finally, briefly, shut down. ## What The Researchers Say, Exactly To put skin on the theoretical bones, here’s what each thinker contributes specifically to the plight of the underestimated rage: - **Harriet Lerner** describes anger as a tool for self-clarity, not a ticket to being labeled. In her work, she notes that when we are quick to forgive, we often bypass the crucial information anger carries about a relationship’s inequality. Her advice is blunt: “Anger is a signal, and one worth listening to.” It is not a hot potato to be passed off; it’s a diagnostic instrument. When your therapist suggests your rage is disproportionate, channel Lerner and ask yourself: *What is this anger trying to name that I’ve been too scared to say?* - **Thich Nhat Hanh** offered the practice of “breathing in, I know that anger is here; breathing out, I am taking good care of my anger.” The key word is *good care*. He did not say “eliminate” or “transcend.” He taught that the first step toward peace is an acknowledgement that is both embodied and nonjudgmental. This is miles away from stuffing anger into a silent retreat. In his community, they’d sometimes allow a mindful “anger room” where you could beat the floor with your fists in conscious presence - not as chaos, but as ritual. - **Karla McLaren** explicitly redefines anger not as a negative emotion but as a necessary, honorable emotion that arises when your boundary has been trampled. She writes that anger contains a gift: the ability to set firm limits with clarity and without guilt. For someone whose rage has been pathologized, this is a watershed. She recommends dialoguing with your anger: “What needs to be protected here? What action is necessary?” The answer is rarely “do nothing and breathe deeper.” This is not armchair theory. It’s a map for navigating therapy rooms that might, with the best intentions, be misreading your nervous system’s most honest output. For ongoing resilience in the face of that stress, some find that an adaptogen like [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) helps the body buffer the cortisol spikes that come from chronic emotion suppression - not as a sedative, but as a way to return your baseline to a place where rage doesn’t feel like a constant emergency. ## The Social Cost of Being “Too Much” There’s a reason you’ve learned to swallow the heat before it leaves your mouth. For anyone who isn’t a straight, white, cisgender man in a position of comfortable authority, rage carries social penalties. If you are a woman, you’re hysterical or unhinged. If you are a person of color, you’re the angry stereotype. If you are queer or trans, your legitimate anger at systemic erasure gets reframed as fragility or oversensitivity. The therapist’s office can replicate these dynamics unless they are acutely aware - and many are not. Imagine the session where you, a Black woman, try to explain the fury of microaggressions at work and you’re asked to consider whether you’re “catastrophizing.” Your hands get cold instantly. Your voice goes flat and you feel a detachment, like you’re watching the scene from the corner of the ceiling. This is dissociation, a brilliant survival mechanism that kicks in when the room meant to witness you instead becomes another site of invalidation. You nod along because the alternative - explaining yet again, doing the emotional labor - is too exhausting. You pay the copay, you leave, and the rage turns inward, curdling into depression or a flare of searing eczema along your jawline that no cream can touch because it’s fed by unspoken words. Thich Nhat Hanh’s practice might hold you here: you can, in the parking lot, place your hand on the burning skin and whisper, “I know you are there, my anger. I will not abandon you.” It’s a private act of reclamation that the clinical space failed to offer. It doesn’t fix the structural betrayal, but it stops you from gaslighting yourself when no one else will stop, either. ## When the Tea Is Not Enough: Befriending the Nervous System You might reach for something, anything, to calm the storm. A cup of [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54H9G?tag=spankyspinola-20) steeped strong can take the jagged edge off, letting your shoulders descend from their perch near your ears. Kava is a root that eases the muscular bracing of anxiety without fuzzing your mind, and there’s no shame in using it. But the tea is a companion, not a solution. The solution lies in retraining your system to recognize that anger is not a fire to be doused with shame, but a weather system to be navigated with skill. Your therapist might underestimate your rage because they were trained to manage emotional intensity as a symptom of dysregulation. Many therapy models are top-down: they want to whip your prefrontal cortex back online so you can “think clearly” again. But rage lives in the brainstem, in the psoas, in the fascia that connects your gut to your vocal cords. It doesn’t care about your thought log. It needs movement, sound, and witness. If you’ve ever punched a mattress and then collapsed into sobbing that felt like a clean lake after a mudslide, you know the difference. The counterintuitive gift here is that you can become the keeper of your own rage’s intelligence. No therapist can monitor your internal barometer twenty-four hours a day. You can learn, through the five-minute practice or through the simple act of humming at a low pitch when you’re seething, to be the container your early caregivers could not be. That is not replacing therapy; it’s insisting that therapy serves your liberation, not your compliance. ## FAQ: Three Questions You Actually Ask **“What if my therapist tells me my anger is just a defense mechanism?”** It might be, but that’s not the whole story. A defense mechanism protects something - usually a vulnerability that hasn’t been safe to express. A therapist who stays only at the “defense” level is like a mechanic who tells you your check engine light is just a light. Yes, it’s a light. But it’s signaling a real issue under the hood. Insist on digging deeper. If your therapist can’t move from labeling your anger to exploring what boundary it’s guarding, consider that a yellow flag. **“Can rage coexist with a spiritual practice?”** Absolutely. In fact, a spiritual practice that has no room for rage is incomplete. Thich Nhat Hanh’s monks trained to greet anger with mindfulness, not to exile it. Many wisdom traditions include fierce deities, prophetic indignation, and sacred howling. The question isn’t whether you feel rage, but whether you allow it to teach you something about injustice and self-protection before you release it. If a spiritual teacher shames your anger, they are protecting their own comfort, not your soul. **“How do I know if my anger is ‘too much’ or just right?”** The intensity of your anger is often proportional to the length of its suppression. If you’ve been silent for thirty years, the first eruptions will feel volcanic. That doesn’t mean the anger is wrong; it means the volume is cranked up because it’s been locked in the basement. The guide is not the volume but the direction: is your anger pointing at a violation, a neglected need, or a pattern of disrespect? If yes, the anger is fitting. The work is to express it in ways that don’t harm you or others, not to question its right to exist. A useful metric from Karla McLaren: anger’s purpose is to restore integrity and safety. If that’s your aim, the rage is giving you wisdom, not just noise. --- There is a version of you that was never allowed to be loud. You learned, early and often, that your frown ruined dinner, that your sharp tone made you unlovable, that your accusations were “crazy-making.” So you became a student of containment, and you did it so well that even the people paid to listen to you sometimes mistake your performance for reality. But underneath the psychotherapized stillness is a voice that still knows the score. It knows that certain apologies were never offered. It knows that certain boundaries were crossed while you were encouraged to see the good intentions of the transgressor. It knows that you have been too generous, too understanding, too quick to soothe others while your own nervous system rang like a fire alarm silenced but not turned off. The anger you sit with on a Tuesday afternoon, alone in your car or in the sterile cube of a session, is not a problem. It is the undersong of a self that still believes in its own dignity. You can stop negotiating it away. You can let it speak - not to terrify, but to inform. And maybe, the next time your therapist subtly suggests you’re overreacting, you’ll feel something new in your body: a steady heat, not a conflagration, a hand placed on your own belly, and a quiet, unshakable reply. “No. This is not too much. This is exactly the point.” For more on navigating emotional storms without bypassing them, a companion piece at [the Oracle Lover](https://theoraclelover.com) explores the body’s wisdom in therapy. Your rage knows where it comes from. The question is whether anyone in the room will finally listen. ## What the research and the body both say In our experience, the most useful sources on this stay close to the body. For a careful clinical view, see the NIH on anger and the autonomic nervous system. If you want to keep going inside this journal, you can read [the assessments hub](/assessments), take a quiet hour with [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit), or stay in the writing through [more articles in this journal](/articles). ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated January 18, 2026. In our work on when your therapist underestimates your rage, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
